content:
What is the reason for us not using the CStr from the stdlib?
1.77 introduced C-string literals that automatically add a \0: c"abc" == b"abc\0"
std::ffi::CStr has been a thing since 1.0. It got moved to core::ffi::CStr only as recently as rust 1.64 however.

Yeah, we couldn't use it before but I'm in favor of adopting it now.

In particular, replacing c_str!("abc") with c"abc" will be great -- this could be a good first issue?

bjorn3 said:

std::ffi::CStr has been a thing since 1.0. It got moved to core::ffi::CStr only as recently as rust 1.64 however.

I see! I didn't know

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Yeah, we couldn't use it before but I'm in favor of adopting it now.

In particular, replacing c_str!("abc") with c"abc" will be great -- this could be a good first issue?

Sounds good
With any luck CStr::from_ptr will be const-stable in the next couple versions, which will be helpful

this could be a good first issue?

Done: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1075
